response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Listen, I've been driving around New York for decades, and let me tell you, the nonsense I see on social media during my hour of scrolling each day drives me absolutely crazy. People share the most ridiculous garbage without thinking twice about it. Just yesterday I saw someone posting that the city was shutting down all delivery services next week. Complete hogwash! I called three of my regular customers to warn them before I realized it was totally made up. Got me all worked up for nothing. The problem is that folks just believe whatever pops up on their screen. I may not have fancy degrees like my parents did, but I know enough to be suspicious of things that sound too wild to be true. When I see something that gets my blood pressure up, which happens more often than I'd like to admit, I've learned to take a breath and actually check if it's real before I start calling everyone I know. My kids keep telling me to look at who's posting things and whether it's from a real news source. That makes sense, even though I still think most of these social media companies should be doing more to stop the lies in the first place. It's their platforms, isn't it? They're making money off this mess. I'm not shy about telling people when they've shared something stupid. Maybe I'm too blunt about it, but someone needs to say something. The other day at church, I told Mrs. Henderson straight up that her post about miracle cures was dangerous nonsense. She didn't appreciate it much, but the truth matters more than hurt feelings. My approach is simple: if something makes you angry or scared, that's exactly when you need to slow down and verify it. Don't just share it because it confirms what you already think. Ask yourself if it makes real sense. Check a couple of different sources. And for heaven's sake, don't get all your information from social media. I still read the actual newspaper when I can, and I talk to real people on my delivery routes. Face-to-face conversation tells you a lot more than some anonymous post online ever will.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 74-year-old self-employed delivery driver with no formal education but educated parents, suggesting exposure to critical thinking despite lack of formal schooling. High extraversion means he's outspoken and social, while very low agreeableness indicates he's blunt and confrontational. High neuroticism means he gets easily anxious and upset, which would make fake content particularly aggravating to him. Low openness suggests he prefers traditional methods and is skeptical of new things. His moderate conscientiousness means he tries to be careful but isn't always systematic. His Christian beliefs and age suggest traditional values. The tone should be direct, somewhat irritable, conversational, and opinionated, reflecting his personality. He'd share practical, street-smart advice based on personal experience rather than academic knowledge. His New York delivery driver background provides concrete examples. His limited social media use (one hour daily) means he encounters fake content but isn't deeply immersed. His hobbies suggest patience in some areas but this contrasts with his low agreeableness and high neuroticism in social situations.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, somewhat irritable, conversational, opinionated, and practical with a working-class New York sensibility
